title: Assignment 1
text:
Both Breiman and Shmueli make great points about properly using data and data analytical tools to properly conduct analyses, form hypotheses, and test hypotheses. 
Breiman focuses more on making an argument against the practice of blindly pursuing highly significant data models without analyzing whether or not the model fits the data and if the model was able to explain the causality of the data. Breiman’s article comes almost a decade earlier, during a period of overemphasis on data models and a neglect of other forms of data analysis. He warns the statistical community that exclusively depending on data models to reach conclusions could lead to improper studies and conclusions and stagnate the world of statistics.
Shmueli writes from a period of time when the overemphasis on data models is not as prevalent, and instead moves the conversation forward, writing about the use of both data modeling and algorithmic modeling hand in hand to better understand data. Similar to Breiman, Shmueli makes distinctions between the two types of modeling and the merits and shortcomings of each, but instead of dividing the methods by the tools like Breiman, she divides them based on the goal achieved (explanation or prediction). In addition, Shmueli supports the use of all statistical and analytical tools in explaining and predicting data.
Both authors agree on the fact that not one method is sufficient in understanding data, forming a hypothesis, and testing the hypothesis. Both champion the idea of using many tools, especially algorithms and machine learning to better help understand correlations and causal relationships.

title: Assignment 2
text:
While working with the data set, some troubles I encountered was that the variables in the data set were not very well defined for someone first looking at the data set without much understanding of the research. A separate readme file explaining what the values meant for each data set would be very helpful.
When dealing with missing values, it is important to know the method of data entry for the specific data set. If the data is entered by hand, an NA means that the value is equal to the value in the previous row, and you can use tidyr::fill() to fill in these cells. If the NA represents a fixed value, you can use dplyr::coalesce() to replace the NA with the value.
To explore the relationship between Tondu and other variables, I could plot the Tondu sentiment distributions in each of the groups.

For the votetsai variable,
I would first use freq() to determine the frequencies of the different Tondu variables (sentiments towards Taiwanese independence). Then we can create a ggplot bar with the frequencies of the different sentiments.

title: EPPS 6323: Lab 3
text:
R Programming (EDA)
(Adapted from Stackoverflow examples) (Objectives: Use plotly, reshape packages, interactive visualization)

library(tidyverse)
library(plotly)
data(iris)
attach(iris)
# Generate plot on three quantitative variables
iris_plot <- plot_ly(iris,
                     x = Sepal.Length,
                     y = Sepal.Width,
                     z = Petal.Length,
                     type = "scatter3d",
                     mode = "markers",
                     size = 0.02)
iris_plot




# Regression object

petal_lm <- lm(Petal.Length ~ 0 + Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width,
               data = iris)
library(reshape2)

#load data

petal_lm <- lm(Petal.Length ~ 0 + Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width,data = iris)

# Setting resolution parameter
graph_reso <- 0.05

#Setup Axis
axis_x <- seq(min(iris$Sepal.Length), max(iris$Sepal.Length), by = graph_reso)
axis_y <- seq(min(iris$Sepal.Width), max(iris$Sepal.Width), by = graph_reso)

# Regression surface
# Rearranging data for plotting
petal_lm_surface <- expand.grid(Sepal.Length = axis_x,Sepal.Width = axis_y,KEEP.OUT.ATTRS = F)
petal_lm_surface$Petal.Length <- predict.lm(petal_lm, newdata = petal_lm_surface)
petal_lm_surface <- acast(petal_lm_surface, Sepal.Width ~ Sepal.Length, value.var = "Petal.Length")
hcolors=c("orange","blue","green")[iris$Species]
iris_plot <- plot_ly(iris,
                     x = ~Sepal.Length,
                     y = ~Sepal.Width,
                     z = ~Petal.Length,
                     text = Species,
                     type = "scatter3d",
                     mode = "markers",
                     marker = list(color = hcolors),
                     size=0.02)
# Add surface
iris_plot <- add_trace(p = iris_plot,
                       z = petal_lm_surface,
                       x = axis_x,
                       y = axis_y,
                       type = "surface",mode = "markers",
                       marker = list(color = hcolors))
iris_plot







Regression object

petal_lm <- lm(Petal.Length ~ 0 + Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width, 
               data = iris)

The problem is that we are treating the dependent variable as a quantitative variable when it is actually a qualitative variable stored as a number for ease of data entry.
To improve the prediction of the dependent variable, we can treat it as a qualitative variable. To perform linear regression, we can create dummy variables to represent each possibility, and perform linear regressions for each of the possibilities.

title: Assignment 2
text:
While working with the data set, some troubles I encountered was that the variables in the data set were not very well defined for someone first looking at the data set without much understanding of the research. A separate readme file explaining what the values meant for each data set would be very helpful.
When dealing with missing values, it is important to recognize the frequency and context of the response. If the frequency is very low (few percent), then we still have enough of the sample to have a sizeable sample. However, if the frequency is higher (10% or greater), then we should look into the reasons for the response and why the question or survey could lead to the lack of a response. In other words, the lack of a response can also be interpreted as a response.
To explore the relationship between Tondu and other variables, I could plot the Tondu sentiment distributions in each of the groups, and see if the distribution levels of a sentiment category vary significantly between each of the variables. In addition, I can run a T-test and determine if Tondu and each variable is dependent.
(how do I make them into groups based on the variable and then make the Tondu distribution and then plot them all on one graph, the Tondu distribution of each sentiment with a bar for each variable)

  ggplot(ew_groups, aes(Tondu)) + 
  geom_bar(aes(fill = interaction(Econ_worse, Tondu)), position="dodge") +
  ylab("Frequency") + 
  xlab("Sentiment towards Taiwanese Independence")




For the votetsai variable, it would be similar. I would determine the frequency of individuals who voted for President Tsai and individuals who did not vote for President Tsai. Then, I would group the data multiple times based on each variable, and then plot them on a bar graph and



create a ggplot bar graph with Tondu as the y axis, and the other variables as the x axis

title: EPPS 6323: Lab 3
text:
R Programming (EDA)
(Adapted from Stackoverflow examples) (Objectives: Use plotly, reshape packages, interactive visualization)

library(tidyverse)
library(plotly)
data(iris)
attach(iris)
# Generate plot on three quantitative variables
iris_plot <- plot_ly(iris,
                     x = Sepal.Length,
                     y = Sepal.Width,
                     z = Petal.Length,
                     type = "scatter3d",
                     mode = "markers",
                     size = 0.02)
iris_plot




# Regression object

petal_lm <- lm(Petal.Length ~ 0 + Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width,
               data = iris)
library(reshape2)

#load data

petal_lm <- lm(Petal.Length ~ 0 + Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width,data = iris)

# Setting resolution parameter
graph_reso <- 0.05

#Setup Axis
axis_x <- seq(min(iris$Sepal.Length), max(iris$Sepal.Length), by = graph_reso)
axis_y <- seq(min(iris$Sepal.Width), max(iris$Sepal.Width), by = graph_reso)

# Regression surface
# Rearranging data for plotting
petal_lm_surface <- expand.grid(Sepal.Length = axis_x,Sepal.Width = axis_y,KEEP.OUT.ATTRS = F)
petal_lm_surface$Petal.Length <- predict.lm(petal_lm, newdata = petal_lm_surface)
petal_lm_surface <- acast(petal_lm_surface, Sepal.Width ~ Sepal.Length, value.var = "Petal.Length")
hcolors=c("orange","blue","green")[iris$Species]
iris_plot <- plot_ly(iris,
                     x = ~Sepal.Length,
                     y = ~Sepal.Width,
                     z = ~Petal.Length,
                     text = Species,
                     type = "scatter3d",
                     mode = "markers",
                     marker = list(color = hcolors),
                     size=0.02)
# Add surface
iris_plot <- add_trace(p = iris_plot,
                       z = petal_lm_surface,
                       x = axis_x,
                       y = axis_y,
                       type = "surface",mode = "markers",
                       marker = list(color = hcolors))
iris_plot







Regression object

petal_lm <- lm(Petal.Length ~ 0 + Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width, 
               data = iris)

title: EPPS 6323 Project Proposal
text:
Area/Topic
As a team, we were inspired by Dr. Vito D'Orazio's work at UTD, where he utilized automated machine learning to create a dynamic forecasting model to predict inter-state violence in Africa. After looking into his work, we decided to further generalize the idea and create a model to predict inter-state affinity: whether two states are allies, trade partners, neutral, have conflicting interests, or hostile towards one another. In order to do this, we will utilize political, social, and economic data.


Research Statement/Method
This study intends to employ machine learning methods to predict affinity between states using social and economic variables. These variables will be dyadic, for example trade flow between a pair of states, and variables unique to a state, such as GDP. The dependent variable, the measure of a state's affinity, could take two forms within this study. Using CAMEO codes and intensity scores found in coded event data, a continuous dependent variable could be derived that represents general affinity between two states. The more complex method would involve using a clustering method to find "affinity communities", which would result in a categorical dependent variable (Pauls 2017).
Instead of limiting the study to a single machine learning method, it would prove insightful to test multiple methods for predictive power against a traditional OLS model, or a LASSO model in the case of the categorical dependent variable. Some of the possible models that could be employed alongside these are random forests, gradient boosted machines, and neural networks. In this way the study can test machine learning methods with varying degrees of complexity.


Data/Variable
This study used data from various sources, including the Data World Bank, International Monetary Fund (IMF), CAMEO event data, UTD Event Data, and other publicly available datasets. All sources were cited appropriately and their references can be found in the bibliography section.
Our analysis considered a range of variables, including dyadic variables such as trade flow between a pair of states, and state-specific variables such as GDP, population, military expenditure, education statistics, gender, and other relevant economic and social factors. In addition, we will use CAMEO codes and intensity scores to calculate a measure of affinity between different countries.

Works Cited
Pauls, Scott & Cranmer, Skyler. (2017). Affinity communities in United Nations voting: Implications for democracy, cooperation, and conflict. Physica A: Statistical Mechanics and its Applications. 484. 10.1016/j.physa.2017.04.177.

# Dataset: USArrests is the sample dataset used in 
# McNeil, D. R. (1977) Interactive Data Analysis. New York: Wiley.
# Murder    numeric Murder arrests (per 100,000)
# Assault   numeric Assault arrests (per 100,000)
# UrbanPop  numeric Percent urban population
# Rape  numeric Rape arrests (per 100,000)
# For each of the fifty states in the United States, the dataset contains the number 
# of arrests per 100,000 residents for each of three crimes: Assault, Murder, and Rape. 
# UrbanPop is the percent of the population in each state living in urban areas.
library(datasets)

text:
Goal: use various lexicons to assess the overall sentiment of various works of text.

library(janeaustenr)
library(stringr)
library(tidytext)
library(tidyverse)

── Attaching packages ─────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse 1.3.2 ──
✔ ggplot2 3.4.0     ✔ purrr   1.0.1
✔ tibble  3.1.8     ✔ dplyr   1.1.0

── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()

library(gutenbergr)
library(textdata)

original_books <- austen_books() %>%
  group_by(book) %>%
  mutate(linenumber = row_number(),
         chapter = cumsum(str_detect(text, 
                                     regex("^chapter [\\divxlc]",
                                           ignore_case = TRUE)))) %>%
  ungroup()

tidy_books <- original_books %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, text)
tidy_books

  filter( n > 600) %>%
  mutate(word = reorder(word, n)) %>%

  geom_col() +
  labs(y = NULL)

nrc_surprise <- get_sentiments("nrc") %>%
  filter(sentiment == "surprise")
tidy_books %>%
  filter(book == "Emma") %>%
  inner_join(nrc_surprise) %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

Joining with `by = join_by(word)`

afinn_oliver <- tidy_oliver %>%
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn")) %>%
  group_by(index = word_number %/% 800) %>%
  summarize(sentiment = sum(value))%>%
  mutate(method = "AFINN")

Joining with `by = join_by(word)`

afinn_oliver %>%
  ggplot(aes(index, sentiment, fill = method)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~method, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y")




Looking at units beyond just words - other units of text can provide a better understanding of the sentiment as a whole.

library(janeaustenr)

library(tidyverse)

library(textdata)

title: NBA Scores from Basketball-Reference
text: In this project, we utilize the BeautifulSoup package in python to scrape summary data about NBA games from basketball-reference.com. The teams, the scores, and the winning team are output in a dataframe, which can be exported as a CSV.
